About: 

Samantha Sutton is a dedicated attorney who advocates for injured individuals, guiding them through the legal process with care. She is passionate about helping clients navigate difficult moments and works diligently to make a meaningful difference in their lives.

Originally from Rock Hill, South Carolina, Samantha earned her Bachelor of Science in Political Science with a minor in English from Clemson University before going on to receive her Juris Doctor from Charlotte School of Law. She began her career in personal injury law in 2017 and has since built a strong foundation representing clients in a wide range of cases. Samantha is also an active member of the South Carolina Bar’s Publications Committee and received an author’s acknowledgment for her contributions to The Law of Automobile Insurance in South Carolina, 8th Edition.

Samantha is driven by a genuine desire to support others during challenging times. Known for her attention to detail and strong communication skills, she ensures her clients feel informed and confident throughout their case.

Outside of the office, Samantha enjoys traveling, practicing yoga, gardening, and participating in a book club. She is also an avid supporter of Clemson sporting events and values spending time with her extended family.

title: Case Page Migration to New Template (SEO General Version)
assignees: Justyna Acosta, Mubeen Dildar-Mia, Sadie de Pinto
description:
In conjunction with SEO and the client's designer, we have created a case page template deemed "SEO General Version" for the Sink law website where we will be migrating all of the case pages (or practice area pages) over the next week or so. Note that there will be a more complex version when the site is rebuilt. Also note that the website most commonly uses the term "practice areas", but the client is moving much more towards the verbiage of "cases we handle".

There are three main categories of pages that will be migrated:
  1. Pages linked on the practice area archive page.
  2. Other case pages with a high user volume that are not linked on the practice area archive page. Those pages will need to be identified by examining GA for data to find out which additional pages are high traffic by users and may need special attention.
  3. General practice area pages that maybe don't get a significant amount of traffic to them. But nonetheless, still are practice area pages should be put into the practice area template for cohesiveness, both for design and SEO purposes.

Method of Migration:

Most of the Practice Area pages follow a very similar format with a main body content and a sidebar (eg https://www.sinklaw.com/car-accident/).
However, there are some pages that have unique formats (eg https://www.sinklaw.com/charleston/car-accident-lawyer/). 
  1. These will all be migrated over initially using scripts. 
  2. We will test migrating one of the unique formats on staging for any major issues, and correct anything major that comes up.
  3. If the pages fall under categories 1 or 2, they will first go into a strict QC for individual page QC and adjusted as needed.
  4. If they fall into category 3, then they will be migrated over as is and spot checked for any major issues.

Migration Strategy:
  • The migration strategy can be found in the Figma design here
  • The client has also asked that we remove the long CTA buttons and change them to text at the bottom of the paragraph text (above the buttons) for their corresponding sections. This will be done manually for all pages in categories 1 and 2.
  • The client has several small changes they want to make to the car accident page, these can be found in a shared spreadsheet with the client. We are working with the client to understand that these are existing elements on the old site. Any changes like these will be made after the initial migration to the new page template. This means that they will see things they feel are odd (like the long buttons) on the live site as we move through a queue to address the pages. However, these are all things that are odd on the site currently, and we don't want to hold back migration to a better SEO and design optimized template before adjusting them.

Context & Objective: The client (https://www.sinklaw.com/) is updating their brand name. We need to remove the ", P.A." from all instances of their name across the website.

Please perform a sitewide sweep to ensure all text, meta data, and structural elements are updated to reflect the new name. Please execute this on the staging environment first for QA before pushing to production.

Developer To-Do List:

Global Elements (Header & Footer)
  • Logo: Ensure the current logo file matches the new branding (if a new logo file is required, see attached assets).

Content & Database (Sitewide Search & Replace)

SEO & Schema Markup
  • Meta Titles: Update the global site title template (e.g., in Yoast/RankMath/custom SEO plugin) to append - George Sink Injury Lawyers instead of the old name.

Forms & Integrations
  • Autoresponders: Update the firm name in all automated email confirmations sent to users when they submit a contact form.

title: Webdesign SEO Alignment
description:
Hey guys! Sink is going to be redesiging their site soon, it's a bit of a complex project with series of phases. Please take some time to review the projec that has been proposed to the client. I'll set a meeting for us to group on this as well and make sure we're all in alignment :)


Sink Law Rough Proposal

Website Redesign for sinklaw.com

Assumptions

  • Branding direction, colors, and fonts will be available to put into the designs starting May 4th, 2026.
  • All designs and reviews will be completed in the times allotted.
  • All projects or conversations regarding copy will be held separately from the design review sessions. Copy at no point will hinder the progress of the designs. It is recommended that designs use Lorem ipsum placeholder copy until the designs are finalized.
  • Designs will include site-ready copy and assets at time of handoff. These do not need to be finalized, but they do need to be in acceptable condition to be used on the site at least temporarily.
  • UI Kit and complete designs for the initial launch phase will be fully prepared and handed off to Clixsy by August 3rd, 2026 EOD.
  • Any requests to update copy/assets can be given during the QC stage, but not sooner.
  • No designs will change after handoff.
  • No copy updates will change the structure of the designs.
  • Any requests for event pages not scheduled in this project will be minimal in intensity and use only existing elements already developed.
  • No pages will be moved between or added to additional phases
  • Post launch phase development will not begin until after the initial launch phase is complete.
  • Case pages will use a simple template during the pre- and initial- launch phases. This will be SEO based and cost will be waived.
  • SEO requests will be honored and implemented into the designs before designs are handed off to Clixsy.
  • Both Clixsy and Sink Law will be comprehensive in website QC during the respective phases, allotting sufficient time to ensure the website is thoroughly reviewed. This will allow maximum efficiency and avoid delays.
  • No design changes will be made after the design handoff. No design changes will be requested during the QC process.
  • Clixsy will take a snapshot of the site just before development begins. At that moment, the site will be on “hold” until the new site is launched. During this hold period, any changes made to the live site will need to be replicated on the new site. Sink Law and any third parties working on the site will record any changes they make during this period and either send in a ticket to our team to replicate on the new site, or maintain their own record to make the change on the site once it is live. The snapshot will be used for migration purposes, and so any changes made during this interim period will likely be lost on the new site.


Phases



Pre Launch Phase
March 18th - July 3rd

During this stage, key pages will be redesigned and developed on the current website theme. These pages will later need to be translated when development of the new site begins.

Stages:
  • Design

title: Prevent Duplicate Webform Submissions
description:
Domain: sinklaw.com

The client is reviewing webform submissions and noticed that some users appear to be creating multiple intakes in Litify from duplicate submissions. They asked whether anything currently prevents multiple submissions from creating multiple Litify intakes and whether other factors could prevent a webform submission from generating an intake.


Keith responded in the thread confirming that there is currently nothing preventing duplicate submissions from triggering the Litify webhook. As a preventative measure, he recommended implementing front-end prevention by disabling the submit button after the first click to reduce accidental double submissions.


The client agreed that this would be a good place to start.


Client Goal:
Reduce duplicate Litify intakes caused by accidental multiple form submissions.


Requirements Checklist

  • Forms that generate Litify intakes prevent multiple submissions caused by repeated clicks during processing

  • Submit button becomes inactive after the initial submission attempt

  • User receives clear visual feedback indicating the form is processing

  • Valid form submissions continue to trigger the Litify webhook and create an intake as expected

    $submission = WPCF7_Submission::get_instance();

title: Recommended Approach for Preventing Duplicate Litify Intakes
description:
Background:
The client is reviewing webform submissions and noticed that some users appear to be creating multiple intakes in Litify from duplicate submissions. 

Keith confirmed in the email thread that there is currently nothing preventing duplicate submissions from triggering the Litify webhook. He suggested three potential approaches: 
  1. Front-end prevention: disable the submit button after the first click to prevent accidental double submissions.
  2. De-duplication logic: track submissions by email (or another identifier) and suppress repeat Litify webhooks.
  3. Litify-side controls: configure Litify to treat Email + Source as a unique identifier and merge/prevent duplicates.
The client responded that Option 1 seems like a good place to start and asked whether they should implement all three or if selecting one option is sufficient.
 
They are still reviewing the data and mentioned that some duplicate contacts appear to have been entered manually rather than through the API.
 
Question:
Before I confirm next steps with the client, do we recommend starting with front-end prevention only, or should we plan for any additional safeguards (such as de-duplication logic or Litify-side configuration)? I've submitted the ticket for front-end prevention, but wanted to follow up on the rest of Trudy's questions.

title: Updating the Logo
description:
Hi!

We got a request from Trudy to update the logo on the George Sink website: https://www.sinklaw.com/

Here's the new logo:



Alternatively, there's a red version we could use, but Trudy and I agreed the white would look best.

Given previous feedback from the dev team, I know this site is new to us and not truly onboarded, but we'd like to do what we can to get some change requests processed in order to demonstrate value to the client and secure the contract at the rate Corey is pitching.

That said, I've been told site changes can be cumbersome due to the half-in nature of the site. Would updating the logo in the header and footer be possible as a change request task? If not, I at least want it on the team's radar so it's queued.

Let me know, and I can get this sent in as a task via email if it's doable. Thank you!
